name: Conjunctivitis / Red Eye
summary: Walk-in evaluation and treat-and-release of uncomplicated infectious or allergic conjunctivitis with vision preserved.

triage: Confirm no vision loss, no severe pain, no photophobia, no recent trauma/chemical exposure
Ask about contact lens use, unilateral vs bilateral, discharge type (watery vs purulent), duration
Screen for fever, recent URI, sick contacts, allergy history
Isolate/seat away from others given contagiousness; perform hand hygiene
nursing: Document visual acuity each eye (Snellen) with current correction
Note discharge, lid swelling, periorbital erythema, preauricular node
Provide tissues; reinforce hand hygiene and avoid eye touching
keyOrders: Penlight/external eye exam for injection pattern, discharge, foreign body, corneal clarity
Fluorescein staining with cobalt blue light if pain/abrasion/contact lens suspected*
Evert lids to exclude retained foreign body if indicated*
Pupillary exam for size, reactivity, symmetry
labs: Conjunctival culture not routinely available in urgent care; defer to ophthalmology if hyperacute/severe* if indicated
Consider rapid adenovirus testing only if available* if indicated
imaging: Defer CT orbit to ER if orbital cellulitis or globe injury suspected
meds: Allergic: olopatadine 0.1-0.2% ophthalmic 1 drop affected eye(s) once-twice daily; add oral cetirizine 10 mg daily
Bacterial (purulent): erythromycin 0.5% ointment QID x5-7d or trimethoprim-polymyxin B 1 drop QID x5-7d
Contact-lens wearer with bacterial signs: fluoroquinolone drop (e.g., ofloxacin 0.3%) covering Pseudomonas*
Viral: supportive care, cool compresses, preservative-free artificial tears QID
Avoid topical steroids in urgent care; defer to ophthalmology*
reassess: Recheck visual acuity stable before discharge
Confirm pain controlled and no worsening photophobia
Verify patient/caregiver can instill drops correctly
disposition: Discharge home for routine viral, bacterial, or allergic conjunctivitis
Refer to ophthalmology within 24-48h if contact-lens-related, herpetic suspicion, or no improvement
Transfer to ER or call 911 if vision loss, severe pain, photophobia with hazy cornea, chemical burn, or suspected orbital cellulitis
discharge: Hand hygiene; discard/replace eye makeup; do not share towels/pillows
Discontinue contact lens use until symptom-free and per provider
Cool compresses and artificial tears for comfort
Return precautions: worsening pain, decreasing vision, light sensitivity, no improvement in 48-72h
followup: PCP if symptoms persist beyond 7-10 days
Ophthalmology for contact-lens, recurrent, or non-resolving cases
School/work return per local guidance once discharge resolved

redFlags: Significant vision loss or marked decrease in acuity
Severe eye pain, photophobia, or ciliary flush suggesting keratitis/uveitis
Hazy or opacified cornea, hypopyon, or fixed/irregular pupil
Periorbital/orbital swelling with proptosis, pain on eye movement, or fever (orbital cellulitis)
Contact-lens wearer with corneal infiltrate or ulcer
compliance: Urgent care is not a freestanding ER; vision-threatening presentations require EMTALA-mindful transfer to ER/ophthalmology
Document visual acuity on every red-eye visit as standard of care
Avoid prescribing topical steroids without ophthalmology involvement
Provide written Texas return precautions and document referral
No controlled substances indicated for routine conjunctivitis

name: Superficial Ocular Foreign Body
summary: Removal of a superficial, non-penetrating conjunctival or corneal foreign body with abrasion care.

triage: Determine mechanism; exclude high-velocity projectile, grinding/hammering metal-on-metal, or blast (penetrating risk)
Screen for vision loss, severe pain, or visible globe deformity
Note time of injury, eye protection use, contact lenses
If chemical or high-velocity penetrating injury suspected, escalate immediately
nursing: Document visual acuity each eye before manipulation
Prepare topical anesthetic, fluorescein strips, irrigation, sterile applicators
Position patient supine with adequate lighting
keyOrders: Penlight and slit-lamp/loupe exam; evert upper and lower lids to find FB
Fluorescein staining to identify abrasion, rust ring, or Seidel sign (positive Seidel = ER)
Foreign body removal: copious saline irrigation, then moistened cotton applicator or 25-27g needle/spud for embedded corneal FB
imaging: No imaging for confirmed superficial FB
Defer CT orbit to ER if intraocular/penetrating FB suspected (do NOT MRI metallic FB)
meds: Prophylactic antibiotic: erythromycin 0.5% ointment QID or polymyxin-trimethoprim drops QID x3-5d
Contact-lens wearers: anti-pseudomonal fluoroquinolone drop (ofloxacin/ciprofloxacin) QID
Oral analgesia: acetaminophen 650-1000 mg or ibuprofen 400-600 mg PRN
Do NOT prescribe topical anesthetic for home use (toxic to cornea)
reassess: Re-stain to confirm FB fully removed and characterize residual abrasion
Confirm no Seidel sign, no anterior chamber abnormality
disposition: Discharge home after successful removal of superficial FB with abrasion care
Refer to ophthalmology within 24h for retained/deep corneal FB, large rust ring, or central visual-axis involvement
Transfer to ER or call 911 if penetrating/intraocular FB, positive Seidel, hyphema, or significant vision loss
discharge: No eye rubbing; no contact lenses until cleared and re-examined
Use prescribed antibiotic and artificial tears; expect foreign-body sensation 24h
Wear eye protection in future high-risk activities
Return precautions: increasing pain, decreasing vision, discharge, photophobia, or no improvement in 24-48h
followup: Recheck in 24-48h (urgent care or PCP) to confirm abrasion healing
Ophthalmology next-day for rust ring removal if not fully cleared

redFlags: High-velocity mechanism (grinding, hammering, blast) suggesting intraocular foreign body
Positive Seidel sign or visible globe laceration/extruding contents
Hyphema, irregular/teardrop pupil, or shallow anterior chamber
Foreign body in central visual axis or deeply embedded/unable to remove
Chemical or thermal injury accompanying the foreign body
compliance: Urgent care manages superficial FB only; suspected penetrating/intraocular injury is an EMTALA-relevant ER transfer
Never dispense topical anesthetic for home use due to corneal toxicity
Avoid MRI when metallic intraocular FB is possible
Document pre- and post-removal visual acuity and informed consent for the procedure
Report work-related eye injuries appropriately and document for workers' compensation per Texas requirements

name: Stye / Hordeolum
summary: Conservative treat-and-release management of a localized eyelid gland infection without orbital involvement.

triage: Confirm localized lid lump without vision change, fever, or spreading facial swelling
Distinguish acute tender hordeolum from chronic non-tender chalazion
Screen for periorbital/orbital signs: proptosis, pain on eye movement, diffuse swelling
Note duration, prior episodes, blepharitis history
nursing: Inspect lid margin, point of tenderness, internal vs external location
Provide warm compress for comfort during wait
keyOrders: External lid exam and lid eversion to locate internal hordeolum
Penlight exam to confirm globe uninvolved and pupil normal
Lid hygiene instruction (dilute baby shampoo or lid scrubs)*
Incision and drainage only if pointing and not resolving — generally defer to ophthalmology* if indicated
labs: Consider wound culture only if drained and atypical/recurrent* if indicated
imaging: Defer CT orbit to ER if orbital/preseptal cellulitis suspected
meds: Primary therapy: warm compresses 10-15 min 3-4x/day plus gentle lid massage
Erythromycin 0.5% ophthalmic ointment to lid margin BID if drainage/secondary conjunctivitis*
Oral analgesia: acetaminophen or ibuprofen PRN
Oral antibiotics (e.g., doxycycline/dicloxacillin) only if early preseptal spread — consider ER/ophthalmology referral*
reassess: Confirm no progression to periorbital/orbital cellulitis
Verify vision unchanged and globe uninvolved
disposition: Discharge home with conservative care for uncomplicated hordeolum
Refer to ophthalmology for persistent chalazion, recurrent lesions, or need for I&D/intralesional steroid
Transfer to ER or call 911 if orbital cellulitis signs: proptosis, painful/restricted eye movement, vision change, fever, or spreading erythema
discharge: Continue warm compresses and lid hygiene; do not squeeze or pop the lesion
Avoid eye makeup and contact lenses until resolved
Most resolve in 1-2 weeks; residual painless nodule may persist as chalazion
Return precautions: increasing swelling/redness, vision change, fever, or pain with eye movement
followup: PCP or ophthalmology if not improving within 1-2 weeks
Ophthalmology for chalazion excision or recurrent styes
Sooner if spreading or systemic symptoms develop

redFlags: Proptosis, pain or restriction with eye movement, or diplopia (orbital cellulitis)
Diffuse spreading periorbital/facial erythema or warmth
Vision change or afferent pupillary defect
Rapidly enlarging mass or recurrent lesion in same site (concern for sebaceous carcinoma)
compliance: Urgent care provides conservative management; orbital cellulitis is an emergency requiring EMTALA-mindful ER transfer
Eyelid I&D and intralesional steroid injection generally exceed routine urgent-care scope — refer to ophthalmology
Document visual acuity and absence of orbital signs
Prescribe oral antibiotics judiciously and only when infection extends beyond a simple stye
